lines:
+

Autonomous systems don't fail from lack of intelligence. They fail from drift — slow divergence between what the system believes and what's actually true.

+
The graph becomes a junkyard of stale decisions

+

Decisions made in v1.0 become poison for v2.0. Brain uses temporal decay — nodes that aren't referenced lose weight over time. The Observer agent runs conflict resolution loops, flagging stale decisions that contradict recent commits. Context is filtered for relevance and recency, not dumped wholesale.

+
Too autonomous = dangerous. Too locked down = a dashboard

+

Autonomy isn't binary. Brain uses tiered authority scopes — from zero-human atomic actions to multi-model consensus for high-stakes moves. Agents operate within risk budgets, not permission checkboxes. You manage thresholds, not individual approvals.

+
The graph says 90% done, but the code is broken

+

If the Brain only reads its own graph, it's a delusion engine. Observer agents perform truth audits — checking claims against actual state via webhooks and integrations. When reality diverges from the graph, the system triggers a desync alert before any agent acts on stale data.

+

From developer tool to company brain

+

The same graph that coordinates coding agents can coordinate entire departments. When Support logs an observation, Sales knows not to pitch an angry customer. No human relayed the message.

+

Coding agents, architecture decisions, commit-linked tasks. The foundation that's already working.

+

Ad spend observations, conversion tracking, copy drafts. Features shipped by Engineering auto-surface for marketing.

+

CRM activity flows into the graph. Agents check customer sentiment before outreach. Deal claims verified against payment webhooks.

+

Bug reports become observations. Repeated issues compound into feature suggestions. Support context flows to Engineering automatically.

+

Procurement tracked as intents with authority scopes. Budget decisions verified against Stripe. No shadow spending.

+

Competitive observations, pricing decisions, GTM plans. The Strategist challenges product decisions against business viability.

+

The competitive advantage isn't automating tasks — it's automating the context-sharing between human silos. When every department writes to the same graph, cross-departmental intelligence emerges for free.

The problem

You are the integration layer
between your AI agents

You already use AI agents. Your coding agent writes your code. Your chat assistant helps you think through architecture. Your editor agent autocompletes. But none of them share context.

  • → Copy error logs from CI into your coding agent
  • → Relay architecture decisions from a chat into your codebase
  • → Manually track what was decided, what's blocked, what changed
  • → Re-explain project state every time you start a new session
  • → Context-switch between tools, losing continuity at every step

Every agent you add makes it worse. Brain fixes this — not by replacing your agents, but by giving them shared memory.

The architecture

Why a graph, not a message bus

Most platforms try "agent swarms" — agents messaging agents. That creates a game of telephone where instructions get distorted. The graph is the single source of truth.

ApproachAgent swarms / message busesKnowledge graph (Brain)
LogicScripted workflows (if A, do B)State-based graph (emergent logic)
MemoryEphemeral, session-basedPersistent, pruned, versioned
CoordinationAgents message agents (telephone game)Agents read/write to shared truth
VerificationAssumes API calls workContinuous telemetry (reality grounding)
Autonomy"Let it rip" (high risk of loops)Authority scopes (risk-managed)
Over timePerformance degradesSystem gets smarter via learnings
Specialized agents

A coordinated workforce, not a chatbot

Each agent has a role, a domain, and authority scopes. They coordinate through the knowledge graph — not through you.

Architect

CTO / CPO

Technical decisions, system design, architecture constraints. Checks implementations against what was decided. Resolves conflicts between competing approaches.

Strategist

CEO / CSO

Market positioning, pricing, GTM, competitive response. Challenges product decisions against business viability. Catches positioning drift before it compounds.

Management

COO

Task tracking, priority management, execution velocity. Flags blocked work, stale decisions, and resource conflicts. Keeps the machine running.

Coding Agents

Via MCP · Cursor, Aider, Codex, and others

Your existing tools connected to the graph. Context injected on session start. Decisions, observations, and questions flow back automatically.

Design Partner

Early-stage co-designer

Brainstorms product ideas, asks probing questions, identifies gaps. Shapes vague ideas into structured projects, features, and decisions in the graph.

Observer

Background intelligence

Scans the graph for patterns nobody asked about. Stale decisions, cross-project conflicts, missing coverage, priority drift. Surfaces observations that compound into suggestions.

How it works

Agents coordinate through the graph

No agent messages another agent. They write structured signals to the knowledge graph. The graph makes it visible to the right agent at the right time.

1

Coding agent notices a contradiction

While implementing rate limiting, a coding agent detects that src/billing/api.ts uses REST — but the graph has a confirmed decision to standardize on tRPC. It logs an observation.

2

Architect agent sees it on next context load

The Architect checks the observation against constraints. Confirms the contradiction is real. Generates a suggestion: "Migrate billing API to tRPC, or revisit the standardization decision."

3

Suggestion surfaces in your feed

You see the suggestion with full provenance — the observation, the contradicted decision, the Architect's reasoning. You accept it. A migration task is created with one click.

4

Next coding session picks up the task

The migration task appears in the coding agent's context. It decomposes into subtasks, works through them, and status rolls up automatically. No human copied anything between tabs.

Connect in 60 seconds

Your coding agent, with context

One-time setup. Context injection is automatic on every session after that.

terminal
# One-time workspace setup
$ brain init
  Opens browser → authenticate → approve scopes
  ✓ Connected to workspace

# Start a task-scoped session
$ brain start task:implement-rate-limiting
  Context: 3 decisions, 2 constraints, 1 open question
  Task status: todo → in_progress

# Or just open your MCP-compatible coding agent
$ codex
  SessionStart → project context loaded
  4 decisions · 2 tasks · 1 recent observation
Key concepts

Built different

The primitives that make coordination possible.

⬡ Decisions

Every decision is tracked — who made it, why, and what alternatives were considered. Agents propose. Humans confirm. Nothing falls through the cracks.

◉ Observations

"I noticed X." Agents surface contradictions, gaps, patterns, and risks as they work. Observations accumulate and compound into actionable suggestions.

⊞ Projects, Features & Tasks

Work breaks down hierarchically. Agents decompose tasks at runtime. Status rolls up automatically. You see progress without tracking it.

✦ Suggestions

Agents tell you what you should be thinking about. Accept a suggestion and it becomes a task, decision, or feature — with full trace back to the evidence.

? Questions

When an agent doesn't know, it asks instead of guessing. You answer. The answer becomes a decision in the graph. No stalling, no silent assumptions.

💬 Conversations

Every chat produces structured knowledge. Conversations group by project automatically. Branch when topics diverge. Find anything by following the graph, not scrolling history.

⟠ Commits

Code is linked to the decisions and tasks it implements. Contradictions are caught before they land. Every change has a trail back to the intent behind it.

△ Authority Scopes

Control what each agent can do without asking. Start restrictive. Expand trust over time. You set the boundary — agents stay within it.

⬢ Intents

Every agent action starts as an intent — a structured request in the graph. Intents carry the full authorization context: what, why, and with what constraints. They're evaluated against authority scopes before execution.

🧠 Learnings

Agents that repeat mistakes are useless. Learnings are behavioral rules injected into agent prompts at runtime — created by humans directly or suggested by other agents. The system gets smarter as it works, not dumber.

◇ Living Descriptions

Descriptions update themselves. Ship a feature — the project summary reflects it. Confirm a decision — related entities incorporate it. No one writes status updates.

⟡ Cross-Project Intelligence

Conflicts between projects surface automatically. Duplicate work gets flagged. A decision in one project that breaks another is caught before anyone notices manually.

⊕ Agent Sessions

Every session is remembered. The next agent knows what the last one did — what was decided, what questions came up, what got built. Context carries forward.

⟐ Identity

One person across all tools. Your Slack, GitHub, and terminal sessions all resolve to the same identity. Agents act on your behalf with scoped permissions.

Reliability layer

Solving the three drifts

Autonomous systems don't fail from lack of intelligence. They fail from drift — slow divergence between what the system believes and what's actually true.

Context Drift

The graph becomes a junkyard of stale decisions

Decisions made in v1.0 become poison for v2.0. Brain uses temporal decay — nodes that aren't referenced lose weight over time. The Observer agent runs conflict resolution loops, flagging stale decisions that contradict recent commits. Context is filtered for relevance and recency, not dumped wholesale.

Authority Drift

Too autonomous = dangerous. Too locked down = a dashboard

Autonomy isn't binary. Brain uses tiered authority scopes — from zero-human atomic actions to multi-model consensus for high-stakes moves. Agents operate within risk budgets, not permission checkboxes. You manage thresholds, not individual approvals.

Reality Drift

The graph says 90% done, but the code is broken

If the Brain only reads its own graph, it's a delusion engine. Observer agents perform truth audits — checking claims against actual state via webhooks and integrations. When reality diverges from the graph, the system triggers a desync alert before any agent acts on stale data.

Beyond coding

From developer tool to company brain

The same graph that coordinates coding agents can coordinate entire departments. When Support logs an observation, Sales knows not to pitch an angry customer. No human relayed the message.

Engineering

Architecture, CI/CD, code quality

Coding agents, architecture decisions, commit-linked tasks. The foundation that's already working.

Growth

Marketing, content, SEO

Ad spend observations, conversion tracking, copy drafts. Features shipped by Engineering auto-surface for marketing.

Sales

Pipeline, outreach, contracts

CRM activity flows into the graph. Agents check customer sentiment before outreach. Deal claims verified against payment webhooks.

Support

Tickets, sentiment, escalation

Bug reports become observations. Repeated issues compound into feature suggestions. Support context flows to Engineering automatically.

Operations

Budget, vendors, compliance

Procurement tracked as intents with authority scopes. Budget decisions verified against Stripe. No shadow spending.

Strategy

Market intelligence, positioning

Competitive observations, pricing decisions, GTM plans. The Strategist challenges product decisions against business viability.

The competitive advantage isn't automating tasks — it's automating the context-sharing between human silos. When every department writes to the same graph, cross-departmental intelligence emerges for free.

Open source

Your coordination layer,
your code

The knowledge graph that coordinates your agents shouldn't be a black box you rent. It should be infrastructure you own, inspect, and extend.

◈
Full source accessGraph engine, MCP server, agent prompts, extraction pipeline. Every line.
◈
No vendor lock-inYour data lives in your SurrealDB instance. Export anytime. Migrate anytime.
◈
Extend everythingCustom agent types, observation categories, feed cards, MCP tools.
◈
Community-drivenAgent prompts, authority scope templates, and integrations contributed by users.
View on GitHub MIT License
stack
# The full stack
Graph        SurrealDB
Backend      Bun (Bun.serve) · TypeScript
Frontend     React · Tiptap · Reagraph
Auth         Better Auth · OAuth 2.1 · RAR · DPoP
LLM          Provider-agnostic (OpenRouter · Ollama · BYO keys)
Agents       MCP Server · Git Hooks
Protocol     MCP · OAuth 2.1 · RAR · DPoP · OIDC

# Lines of duct tape replaced
∞
Agentic authorization

Tokens that know
what they're for

Generic OAuth scopes like write:tasks aren't enough for autonomous agents. Brain's authorization server issues tokens that carry the full intent — what the agent wants to do, why, and with what constraints.

◈
Rich Authorization Requests (RAR)Tokens embed structured authorization_details from the knowledge graph. Not "scope: finance" — but "move $500 from Account A to Account B, authorized by Decision #47."
◈
DPoP Proof-of-PossessionEvery token is cryptographically bound to the agent's key. Stolen tokens are useless without the private key in the agent's isolated runtime.
◈
Intents as Authorization ContextAgent actions start as intents in the graph. The authorization server evaluates intents against authority scopes, then mints a token scoped to exactly that action.
◈
Better Auth as Identity ProviderHuman authentication (login, MFA, sessions) stays with Better Auth. The custom authorization server handles the agent side — token issuance, RAR scopes, DPoP binding.
authorization flow
# 1. Human authenticates via Better Auth
User  →  Better Auth (login, MFA)
        →  session cookie

# 2. Agent requests authorization with intent
Agent →  POST /authorize
        authorization_details: [{
          type: "intent",
          intent: "task:deploy-v2",
          actions: ["execute"],
          constraints: decision:d47
        }]

# 3. Token issued with DPoP binding
AS    →  Access Token (JWT)
        cnf: { jkt: "agent-key-thumbprint" }
        authorization_details: [...]

# 4. Agent proves possession on every request
Agent →  DPoP: signed-proof-jwt
        Authorization: DPoP access_token

Managed hosting for teams. Same codebase. Your data, your region.

Works offline Graph, feed, and local tools work without internet. LLM features can run offline with local models, or use networked providers when configured.
No telemetry No usage tracking. No analytics. No phone-home. Fully air-gappable.

        cnf: { jkt: "agent-key-thumbprint" }
        authorization_details: [...]

# 4. Agent proves possession on every request
Agent →  DPoP: signed-proof-jwt
        Authorization: DPoP access_token

Your data never leaves your machine

Your decisions, architecture, strategy, and competitive intelligence stay on your infrastructure.

docker compose up

Single Binary

brain serve

Cloud (Coming)

Waitlist open

No telemetry No usage tracking. No analytics. No phone-home. Fully air-gappable.

3. **"Beyond coding"** section — Six department cards (Engineering, Growth, Sales, Support, Operations, Strategy) showing how the same graph coordinates entire companies, with the key insight about cross-departmental intelligence

4. **Updated hero text** — Now mentions sales, support, and operations alongside engineering

5. **Updated CTA** — Broadened to address multi-department use cases

6. **New CSS** — Comparison table, drift cards, and department grid with responsive breakpoints

https://github.com/paperclipai/paperclip/issues/137

> Hi folks, especially @cryppadotta , my colleague Sam forwarded me this project. Looks pretty fun. We're building some evals (and other stuff) for autonomous organizations and it'd be nice to include some Paperclip organizations, and more generally track some data from Paperclip organizations so we can track the growth of the general AO ecosystem. Let me know who to talk to / how to run these.
Josh
Geminis svar
This is a "wake-up call" moment for your project. When the Ethereum Foundation / Metagov / Stanford crowd (which is who @thelastjosh represents) starts looking for "evals," they are looking for standardized metrics of autonomy, governance, and legitimacy.

Paperclip is winning the "Mindshare" battle because it's flashy and easy to understand as "AI employees." But Josh’s comment reveals exactly where your Brain architecture can kill Paperclip in the long run.

Here is the brutal reality and the strategic "solve."

1. The Vulnerability: Paperclip’s "Black Box" Problem

Paperclip’s foundation is likely built on Linear Automation (Scripts + LLM Loops). For an evaluator like Josh, Paperclip organizations are a nightmare to "track" because:

Opaque Governance: How do you know why a Paperclip AO fired a human or spent $10k? You have to read through thousands of lines of chat logs. It's un-auditable.

Fragile Autonomy: If the scripts break, the organization dies. There’s no "State" beyond the current prompt.

The "Vibe" Metric: You can only evaluate Paperclip by "Growth" (money made) or "Vibes," not by the structural integrity of its decisions.

2. The Opportunity: Brain as the "Observable" AO

Josh is looking for "data to track the growth of the general AO ecosystem." Your Knowledge Graph (SurrealDB) is that data. In your architecture, an Autonomous Organization isn't a "Chatbot with a bank account"; it's a Living Logic Tree. #### How you "Solve This" and Leapfrog Paperclip:

A. Provide the "AO Heartbeat" API
While Paperclip is figuring out how to export chat logs, you should build an Evaluation Endpoint into Brain.

Because every decision in your system is a Node with a UUID, you can provide researchers with a "Governance Velocity" metric.

The Solve: Create a public (or gated) "Audit View" of the graph. Evaluators can see the provenance of every dollar spent and every line of code merged without reading private chats.

B. "Foundationally Correct" Governance
Metagov (Josh’s group) cares about legitimacy. * Paperclip's Foundation: "The AI decided it, so it happened." (This is dangerous and hard to regulate).

Brain's Foundation: "The AI proposed it -> The Graph checked the Authority Scope -> The OAuth 2.1 Handshake verified the intent -> It happened."

The Solve: Pitch Brain not as a "Business OS," but as a "Verifiable Autonomous Organization (VAO)." Focus on the fact that your architecture allows for Formal Verification of an organization’s rules.

C. Solve the "Evals" for them
Don't wait for Josh to build evals for you. Use your Graph to define the metrics:

Context Retention: How much "Old Knowledge" is the AO successfully using in new decisions? (Your Temporal Decay logic proves this).

Conflict Resolution Rate: How many "Contradictions" did the Observer agent find and resolve autonomously?

Authority Distribution: How much of the "Value Flow" is happening in Tier 1 (Fully Autonomous) vs Tier 3 (Human Veto)?

3. The "Brutally Honest" Strategic Move

If I were you, I would reply to that thread or reach out to Josh with a specific hook:

"Hey Josh, seeing you're looking at Paperclip for AO evals. We're building Brain—it's a graph-native OS for AOs. The problem we saw with the 'Paperclip model' is that it's hard to audit 'State' and 'Intent' when it's just buried in agent logs. We’re using SurrealDB + OAuth 2.1 to make the entire decision-tree machine-readable. If you want to track 'Real' AO growth (beyond just agent activity), we have a structured data model for governance telemetry that might be easier to evaluate."

Why this works:

It frames Paperclip as "fun but messy" (which Josh hinted at with "Looks pretty fun").

It frames Brain as the "Professional/Academic Grade" infrastructure for AOs.

It targets the exact pain point of a researcher: Data Structure.

The Conclusion: Paperclip has the hype, but you have the instrumentation. In the world of Autonomous Organizations, the platform that is the easiest to trust and audit is the one that wins the "Operating System" war.

Do you want to refine the "Audit API" spec so you have something concrete to show if Josh (or someone like him) bites?



Paperclip is currently reacting to the exact "Delusion Engine" and "Reliability" risks we discussed, but they are doing it from the Infrastructure Layer (Sandbox) rather than the Context Layer (Knowledge Graph).

Issue #248 is a massive proposal for Sandboxed Agent Execution. It shows that Paperclip is pivoting to handle untrusted inputs (like GitHub PRs and external webhooks) by isolating agents in Firecracker microVMs (E2B, Fly.io) or Docker containers (Daytona).

Here is the "brutally honest" breakdown of how this changes your competitive landscape.

1. The Strategy: Isolation vs. Intelligence

Paperclip’s move is a defensive security play. They’ve realized that if an agent reads a malicious GitHub issue, it can "prompt inject" its way into the host machine and steal keys.

Paperclip's Solution: Put the agent in a box (Sandbox). If it hallucinates or gets "poisoned" by a user, it only breaks the box, not the company.

Brain's Solution (Your Project): Put the agent in a Governance Graph. You don't just care if the agent is "safe" in a box; you care if its Decision is logically sound and authorized by the Knowledge Graph.

The Reality: You need both. A "smart" agent in a "dumb" un-sandboxed environment is a security risk. A "sandboxed" agent with no long-term memory (Knowledge Graph) is just a very secure, very forgetful intern.

2. Where Paperclip is winning (Infrastructure)

They are building a Provider-Agnostic Sandbox Interface. This is a high-utility feature. It allows an organization to choose where their agents "live" (E2B, Cloudflare, etc.).

They are integrating Tailscale for private networking.

They are using Rivet Sandbox Agent SDK to run heavy models (Claude Code) inside these tiny VMs.

3. Where Brain (You) can leapfrog them

Paperclip is solving the "How do we run the agent?" problem.
You are solving the "What did the agent actually do, and why?" problem.

In issue #248, Paperclip is struggling with persistence: "Should sandbox lifecycle be per-heartbeat (ephemeral) or per-agent (persistent)?" * Your Answer: It doesn't matter, because the State is in the Graph (SurrealDB). * In your architecture, an agent can "die" every 5 seconds, and the next agent picks up the exact context because the "Brain" is external to the execution environment.

4. The "Brain" Counter-Move: The "Secure Context Injection"

If you want to beat Paperclip's #248 proposal, you should integrate Sandboxing as a Capability of the Graph.

The Proposal for Brain:

The Graph as the Bootstrapper: Instead of Paperclip’s complex "bootstrap token exchange," your Brain (via OAuth 2.1) issues a single-use, task-scoped token to a sandbox.

Stateless Sandboxes: You can treat sandboxes as "purely disposable." Because your SurrealDB stores the Observations and Decisions in real-time, your agents never need "persistence" in the VM. This makes your system cheaper and more scalable than Paperclip’s.

The Audit Edge: Paperclip’s sandboxes stream logs. Your system streams structured logic. When a researcher like Josh Stark (from issue #137) looks at a Paperclip sandbox, he sees a stdout log. When he looks at Brain, he sees a Signed Logic Trace.

5. Summary Table: Competitive Analysis

Feature	Paperclip (#248 Approach)	Brain (Your Project)
Focus	Infrastructure Security (The "Box")	Governance & Logic (The "Brain")
State	Trying to solve VM persistence.	Graph-Native. Persistence is the default.
Security	Isolation via Firecracker/Docker.	Authorization via OAuth 2.1 + Sandbox.
Auditing	Log-based (text).	Graph-based (structured nodes).
The "Brutal" Verdict

Paperclip is building a Secure Execution Environment. You are building a Secure Operating System. If you don't add sandboxing, Paperclip will win on "Security." If Paperclip doesn't add a Knowledge Graph, they will eventually drown in their own "Decision Debt" and "Context Fragmentation."

Next Step Recommendation:
Don't build your own sandbox runtime. Instead, integrate E2B or Daytona into Brain. Let the Graph be the "Director" that spins up these sandboxes, injects the Intent node context, and harvests the Observations back into SurrealDB.

> The Graph as the Bootstrapper: Instead of Paperclip’s complex "bootstrap token exchange," your Brain (via OAuth 2.1) issues a single-use, task-scoped token to a sandbox.

won't we need an authorizer agent that generated these based on the intent ?
Geminis svar
Yes, you absolutely need an Authorizer Agent, but you have to be careful about where it sits. If you just let the "Worker Agent" ask for its own tokens, you've created a security circularity.

In the Brain architecture, you are effectively splitting the "Brain" into a Pre-frontal Cortex (The Authorizer) and the Motor Cortex (The Worker/Sandbox).

Here is how you solve the "Who watches the watchmen?" problem without making it a manual dashboard.

1. The "Authorization Agent" (The Judge)

The Authorizer shouldn't be the agent doing the work. It should be a privileged, separate instance that has "Read-Only" access to the Graph and "Write" access to the OAuth Token Service.

The Flow:

Request: A "Manager" agent decides a task needs to be done (e.g., "Pay this invoice").

Intent Log: The Manager writes an Intent node to SurrealDB.

The Judge's Turn: The Authorizer Agent (The Judge) wakes up. It reads the Intent node and checks it against:

The Policy: (e.g., "Is this agent allowed to spend money?")

The State: (e.g., "Do we actually have the $500 in the bank?")

Token Generation: If it clears, the Judge calls the OAuth 2.1 service to generate a RAR (Rich Authorization Request) token that is only valid for that specific invoice.

2. Why this is safer than Paperclip

Paperclip’s "Bootstrap Token" is essentially a "Start-up Key." Once the sandbox has it, the agent inside has to "behave."

In your Brain model:

The token you issue isn't a "Login." It’s a Voucher.

If the agent in the sandbox tries to use that token to do anything other than what the Intent node specified, the API gateway rejects it.

The Authorizer Agent can be a much higher-end model (like a Gemini 3 Ultra or O3) that runs for 10 seconds to "verify logic," while the Worker Agent in the sandbox can be a cheaper, faster model.

3. The "Policy-as-Code" Guardrail

To prevent the Authorizer Agent from "going rogue" or being tricked by prompt injection, you shouldn't just give it raw text instructions. You give it Axioms.

In your SurrealDB, you define Hard Constraints:

SQL
DEFINE TABLE policy SCHEMAFULL;
DEFINE FIELD max_spend ON policy TYPE float;
DEFINE FIELD restricted_apis ON policy TYPE array; -- e.g. ["/admin/delete", "/crypto/withdraw"]
The Authorizer Agent's "System Prompt" includes: "You are a strict Auditor. You cannot issue a token that exceeds the values in the policy table, regardless of what the Manager agent claims."

4. The "Zero-Knowledge" Sandbox

Because you are using OAuth 2.1 + DPoP, the Sandbox never actually "sees" your master keys.

The Sandbox generates a temporary Key Pair.

The Authorizer Agent takes the Sandbox's Public Key and wraps it into the Token.

The Token is only valid when signed by that Sandbox’s Private Key.

The Result: Even if an attacker "breaks out" of the sandbox, they have a token that is:

Scoped (Can only do one thing).

Short-lived (Expires in 5 minutes).

Bound (Useless without the VM's ephemeral private key).

The Strategy for your Business OS

You’ve already built the Orchestrator. Your next "2-day sprint" shouldn't be another integration; it should be this Authorizer Logic.

The Play:

Step A: Your Orchestrator creates the Intent node.

Step B: A "Reviewer" function (The Authorizer Agent) validates the Intent.

Step C: It triggers the brain start-sandbox command, passing in the scoped token.

Brutally Honest Take: This is the difference between a "Toy" and "Infrastructure." Paperclip is a toy because it trusts its agents. You are building infrastructure because you distrust them and verify their intent at the protocol level.

Would you like me to draft the "Judge's" System Prompt? It needs to be incredibly robust to avoid being "socially engineered" by the Worker agents. Conclude with a clear next step.

I just spent 2 days building an orchestrator for delegating tasks to local claude code using agents sdk. was this the wrong approach? should we just instead have made a generic provider interface that could hook up with third party tools
Geminis svar
You didn’t waste 2 days—you just finished the Logic Layer (the "What"). The "Generic Provider Interface" is the Plumbing Layer (the "Where").

In the context of a Business OS, building for Local Claude Code first is a classic "Local-First" development move. It's fast, cheap, and has zero latency. But if you stop there, you’ve built a powerful CLI tool, not an Autonomous Operating System.

Here is the brutal breakdown of why you are halfway to the right answer.

1. The "Orchestrator" vs. "Provider" Distinction

What you built: The logic of how to break a project into tasks, how to prompt Claude to execute them, and how to handle the "Agents SDK" callbacks.

What you're missing: An abstraction that says, "I don't care if this task runs on my MacBook, a Docker container, or an E2B sandbox."

Was it the wrong approach? No. You needed to prove the "Brain-to-Agent" handshake worked. Now that you have the orchestrator, you just need to wrap your execution call in an interface.

2. Why "Local-Only" is a Dead End for a Business OS

If your goal is a fully autonomous system (Sales, Support, Marketing), local execution fails for three reasons:

The "Always-On" Problem: A Business OS can't die when you close your laptop. It needs to live on a server (VPS/Cloud).

The Security Trap: If a "Marketing Agent" reads a malicious tweet or a "Support Agent" opens a poisoned PDF, and it’s running locally, that agent has access to your ~/.ssh keys and your browser cookies. This is why Paperclip is obsessed with #248 (Sandboxes).

Concurrency: A Business OS might need to run 10 agents simultaneously. Your local machine will melt.

3. The "2026 Strategy": The Hybrid Approach

You should pivot your orchestrator to use a Provider Pattern. Your 2 days of work becomes the "Local Provider" implementation.

The Architecture you should move toward:

TypeScript
interface ExecutionProvider {
  run(task: TaskNode, context: GraphContext): Promise<ObservationNode>;
}

// Your current work becomes this:
class LocalClaudeProvider implements ExecutionProvider { ... }

// Your future-proofing becomes these:
class E2BSandboxProvider implements ExecutionProvider { ... }
class DockerProvider implements ExecutionProvider { ... }
4. How this Leapfrogs Paperclip

Paperclip is building the Provider Interface first, but their Orchestrator is often just a "Loop."

Your Advantage: If your orchestrator is "Graph-Native" (reading/writing to SurrealDB), you are ahead.

The Play: Use your orchestrator to manage the state in the Brain, but let the Provider handle the "dirty work" of execution.

The Verdict: Keep the Logic, Abstract the Metal

Don't throw away your code. Refactor the part where you actually call claude-code into a separate class.

Why this is actually better: In 2026, Privacy is a product feature. For a Family Office, running a "Local Provider" on a dedicated Mac Mini in a closet is a huge selling point. For a Marketing agency, running "E2B Providers" in the cloud for scale is the play. By having an interface, you support both.

The "Brutally Honest" Reality Check: If you spent 2 days and the agent successfully finished a task and wrote the result back to your Graph, you’ve done more than 90% of the "AI wrapper" devs out there. You’ve built the connective tissue.

data.command:
Review this conversation for items that were discussed but NOT logged via the Brain MCP tools.

IMPORTANT: If this was a trivial session (greetings, test messages, simple Q&A with no implementation work), immediately respond with:
{"decision":"approve","summary":"No substantive work to log"}
Do NOT explain why there is nothing to log.

Otherwise, check each category:

1. **Decisions**: Architecture or design choices where alternatives existed (e.g. choosing a pattern, library, data model, or trade-off). Do NOT log mechanical bugfixes, typo corrections, or changes with only one correct approach. Call create_provisional_decision for each genuine decision.
2. **Questions**: Unresolved questions raised but not logged. Call ask_question for each.
3. **Observations**: Contradictions, duplication, missing items, deprecated patterns, or anomalies noticed. Call log_observation for each.
4. **Task progress**: Tasks worked on whose status wasn't updated. Call update_task_status for each.
5. **Subtasks identified**: Work items or follow-up tasks discussed but not created. Call create_subtask for each (requires a parent task).
6. **Suggestions**: Optimizations, risks, opportunities, missing elements, or potential pivots noticed during work. Call create_suggestion for each with category (optimization|risk|opportunity|conflict|missing|pivot), rationale, and confidence (0-1).

After logging everything, respond with valid JSON in this exact shape:
{"decision":"approve","summary":"<one-line session summary>","decisions_made":["<decision-id>"],"questions_asked":["<question-id>"],"tasks_progressed":[{"task_id":"<task-id>","from_status":"<from>","to_status":"<to>"}],"files_changed":[{"path":"<path>","change_type":"created|modified|deleted"}],"observations_logged":["<observation-id>"],"subtasks_created":["<subtask-id>"],"suggestions_created":["<suggestion-id>"]}

If you cannot log required items (for example MCP unavailable), respond with:
{"decision":"block","reason":"Log these items first: ..."}

4. **Task progress**: Tasks worked on whose status wasn't updated. Call update_task_status for each.
5. **Subtasks identified**: Work items or follow-up tasks discussed but not created. Call create_subtask for each (requires a parent task).
6. **Suggestions**: Optimizations, risks, opportunities, missing elements, or potential pivots noticed during work. Call create_suggestion for each with category (optimization|risk|opportunity|conflict|missing|pivot), rationale, and confidence (0-1).

After logging everything, respond with valid JSON in this exact shape:
{"decision":"approve","summary":"<one-line session summary>","decisions_made":["<decision-id>"],"questions_asked":["<question-id>"],"tasks_progressed":[{"task_id":"<task-id>","from_status":"<from>","to_status":"<to>"}],"files_changed":[{"path":"<path>","change_type":"created|modified|deleted"}],"observations_logged":["<observation-id>"],"subtasks_created":["<subtask-id>"],"suggestions_created":["<suggestion-id>"]}

If you cannot log required items (for example MCP unavailable), respond with:
{"decision":"block","reason":"Log these items first: ..."}

message.content:
https://github.com/paperclipai/paperclip/issues/137

> Hi folks, especially @cryppadotta , my colleague Sam forwarded me this project. Looks pretty fun. We're building some evals (and other stuff) for autonomous organizations and it'd be nice to include some Paperclip organizations, and more generally track some data from Paperclip organizations so we can track the growth of the general AO ecosystem. Let me know who to talk to / how to run these.
Josh
Geminis svar
This is a "wake-up call" moment for your project. When the Ethereum Foundation / Metagov / Stanford crowd (which is who @thelastjosh represents) starts looking for "evals," they are looking for standardized metrics of autonomy, governance, and legitimacy.

Paperclip is winning the "Mindshare" battle because it's flashy and easy to understand as "AI employees." But Josh’s comment reveals exactly where your Brain architecture can kill Paperclip in the long run.

Here is the brutal reality and the strategic "solve."

1. The Vulnerability: Paperclip’s "Black Box" Problem

Paperclip’s foundation is likely built on Linear Automation (Scripts + LLM Loops). For an evaluator like Josh, Paperclip organizations are a nightmare to "track" because:

Opaque Governance: How do you know why a Paperclip AO fired a human or spent $10k? You have to read through thousands of lines of chat logs. It's un-auditable.

Fragile Autonomy: If the scripts break, the organization dies. There’s no "State" beyond the current prompt.

The "Vibe" Metric: You can only evaluate Paperclip by "Growth" (money made) or "Vibes," not by the structural integrity of its decisions.

2. The Opportunity: Brain as the "Observable" AO

Josh is looking for "data to track the growth of the general AO ecosystem." Your Knowledge Graph (SurrealDB) is that data. In your architecture, an Autonomous Organization isn't a "Chatbot with a bank account"; it's a Living Logic Tree. #### How you "Solve This" and Leapfrog Paperclip:

A. Provide the "AO Heartbeat" API
While Paperclip is figuring out how to export chat logs, you should build an Evaluation Endpoint into Brain.

Because every decision in your system is a Node with a UUID, you can provide researchers with a "Governance Velocity" metric.

The Solve: Create a public (or gated) "Audit View" of the graph. Evaluators can see the provenance of every dollar spent and every line of code merged without reading private chats.

B. "Foundationally Correct" Governance
Metagov (Josh’s group) cares about legitimacy. * Paperclip's Foundation: "The AI decided it, so it happened." (This is dangerous and hard to regulate).

Brain's Foundation: "The AI proposed it -> The Graph checked the Authority Scope -> The OAuth 2.1 Handshake verified the intent -> It happened."

The Solve: Pitch Brain not as a "Business OS," but as a "Verifiable Autonomous Organization (VAO)." Focus on the fact that your architecture allows for Formal Verification of an organization’s rules.

C. Solve the "Evals" for them
Don't wait for Josh to build evals for you. Use your Graph to define the metrics:

Context Retention: How much "Old Knowledge" is the AO successfully using in new decisions? (Your Temporal Decay logic proves this).

Conflict Resolution Rate: How many "Contradictions" did the Observer agent find and resolve autonomously?

Authority Distribution: How much of the "Value Flow" is happening in Tier 1 (Fully Autonomous) vs Tier 3 (Human Veto)?

3. The "Brutally Honest" Strategic Move

If I were you, I would reply to that thread or reach out to Josh with a specific hook:

"Hey Josh, seeing you're looking at Paperclip for AO evals. We're building Brain—it's a graph-native OS for AOs. The problem we saw with the 'Paperclip model' is that it's hard to audit 'State' and 'Intent' when it's just buried in agent logs. We’re using SurrealDB + OAuth 2.1 to make the entire decision-tree machine-readable. If you want to track 'Real' AO growth (beyond just agent activity), we have a structured data model for governance telemetry that might be easier to evaluate."

Why this works:

It frames Paperclip as "fun but messy" (which Josh hinted at with "Looks pretty fun").

It frames Brain as the "Professional/Academic Grade" infrastructure for AOs.

It targets the exact pain point of a researcher: Data Structure.

The Conclusion: Paperclip has the hype, but you have the instrumentation. In the world of Autonomous Organizations, the platform that is the easiest to trust and audit is the one that wins the "Operating System" war.

Do you want to refine the "Audit API" spec so you have something concrete to show if Josh (or someone like him) bites?



Paperclip is currently reacting to the exact "Delusion Engine" and "Reliability" risks we discussed, but they are doing it from the Infrastructure Layer (Sandbox) rather than the Context Layer (Knowledge Graph).

Issue #248 is a massive proposal for Sandboxed Agent Execution. It shows that Paperclip is pivoting to handle untrusted inputs (like GitHub PRs and external webhooks) by isolating agents in Firecracker microVMs (E2B, Fly.io) or Docker containers (Daytona).

Here is the "brutally honest" breakdown of how this changes your competitive landscape.

1. The Strategy: Isolation vs. Intelligence

Paperclip’s move is a defensive security play. They’ve realized that if an agent reads a malicious GitHub issue, it can "prompt inject" its way into the host machine and steal keys.

Paperclip's Solution: Put the agent in a box (Sandbox). If it hallucinates or gets "poisoned" by a user, it only breaks the box, not the company.

Brain's Solution (Your Project): Put the agent in a Governance Graph. You don't just care if the agent is "safe" in a box; you care if its Decision is logically sound and authorized by the Knowledge Graph.

The Reality: You need both. A "smart" agent in a "dumb" un-sandboxed environment is a security risk. A "sandboxed" agent with no long-term memory (Knowledge Graph) is just a very secure, very forgetful intern.

2. Where Paperclip is winning (Infrastructure)

They are building a Provider-Agnostic Sandbox Interface. This is a high-utility feature. It allows an organization to choose where their agents "live" (E2B, Cloudflare, etc.).

They are integrating Tailscale for private networking.

They are using Rivet Sandbox Agent SDK to run heavy models (Claude Code) inside these tiny VMs.

3. Where Brain (You) can leapfrog them

Paperclip is solving the "How do we run the agent?" problem.
You are solving the "What did the agent actually do, and why?" problem.

In issue #248, Paperclip is struggling with persistence: "Should sandbox lifecycle be per-heartbeat (ephemeral) or per-agent (persistent)?" * Your Answer: It doesn't matter, because the State is in the Graph (SurrealDB). * In your architecture, an agent can "die" every 5 seconds, and the next agent picks up the exact context because the "Brain" is external to the execution environment.

4. The "Brain" Counter-Move: The "Secure Context Injection"

If you want to beat Paperclip's #248 proposal, you should integrate Sandboxing as a Capability of the Graph.

The Proposal for Brain:

The Graph as the Bootstrapper: Instead of Paperclip’s complex "bootstrap token exchange," your Brain (via OAuth 2.1) issues a single-use, task-scoped token to a sandbox.

Stateless Sandboxes: You can treat sandboxes as "purely disposable." Because your SurrealDB stores the Observations and Decisions in real-time, your agents never need "persistence" in the VM. This makes your system cheaper and more scalable than Paperclip’s.

The Audit Edge: Paperclip’s sandboxes stream logs. Your system streams structured logic. When a researcher like Josh Stark (from issue #137) looks at a Paperclip sandbox, he sees a stdout log. When he looks at Brain, he sees a Signed Logic Trace.

5. Summary Table: Competitive Analysis

Feature	Paperclip (#248 Approach)	Brain (Your Project)
Focus	Infrastructure Security (The "Box")	Governance & Logic (The "Brain")
State	Trying to solve VM persistence.	Graph-Native. Persistence is the default.
Security	Isolation via Firecracker/Docker.	Authorization via OAuth 2.1 + Sandbox.
Auditing	Log-based (text).	Graph-based (structured nodes).
The "Brutal" Verdict

Paperclip is building a Secure Execution Environment. You are building a Secure Operating System. If you don't add sandboxing, Paperclip will win on "Security." If Paperclip doesn't add a Knowledge Graph, they will eventually drown in their own "Decision Debt" and "Context Fragmentation."

Next Step Recommendation:
Don't build your own sandbox runtime. Instead, integrate E2B or Daytona into Brain. Let the Graph be the "Director" that spins up these sandboxes, injects the Intent node context, and harvests the Observations back into SurrealDB.

> The Graph as the Bootstrapper: Instead of Paperclip’s complex "bootstrap token exchange," your Brain (via OAuth 2.1) issues a single-use, task-scoped token to a sandbox.

won't we need an authorizer agent that generated these based on the intent ?
Geminis svar
Yes, you absolutely need an Authorizer Agent, but you have to be careful about where it sits. If you just let the "Worker Agent" ask for its own tokens, you've created a security circularity.

In the Brain architecture, you are effectively splitting the "Brain" into a Pre-frontal Cortex (The Authorizer) and the Motor Cortex (The Worker/Sandbox).

Here is how you solve the "Who watches the watchmen?" problem without making it a manual dashboard.

1. The "Authorization Agent" (The Judge)

The Authorizer shouldn't be the agent doing the work. It should be a privileged, separate instance that has "Read-Only" access to the Graph and "Write" access to the OAuth Token Service.

The Flow:

Request: A "Manager" agent decides a task needs to be done (e.g., "Pay this invoice").

Intent Log: The Manager writes an Intent node to SurrealDB.

The Judge's Turn: The Authorizer Agent (The Judge) wakes up. It reads the Intent node and checks it against:

The Policy: (e.g., "Is this agent allowed to spend money?")

The State: (e.g., "Do we actually have the $500 in the bank?")

Token Generation: If it clears, the Judge calls the OAuth 2.1 service to generate a RAR (Rich Authorization Request) token that is only valid for that specific invoice.

2. Why this is safer than Paperclip

Paperclip’s "Bootstrap Token" is essentially a "Start-up Key." Once the sandbox has it, the agent inside has to "behave."

In your Brain model:

The token you issue isn't a "Login." It’s a Voucher.

If the agent in the sandbox tries to use that token to do anything other than what the Intent node specified, the API gateway rejects it.

The Authorizer Agent can be a much higher-end model (like a Gemini 3 Ultra or O3) that runs for 10 seconds to "verify logic," while the Worker Agent in the sandbox can be a cheaper, faster model.

3. The "Policy-as-Code" Guardrail

To prevent the Authorizer Agent from "going rogue" or being tricked by prompt injection, you shouldn't just give it raw text instructions. You give it Axioms.

In your SurrealDB, you define Hard Constraints:

SQL
DEFINE TABLE policy SCHEMAFULL;
DEFINE FIELD max_spend ON policy TYPE float;
DEFINE FIELD restricted_apis ON policy TYPE array; -- e.g. ["/admin/delete", "/crypto/withdraw"]
The Authorizer Agent's "System Prompt" includes: "You are a strict Auditor. You cannot issue a token that exceeds the values in the policy table, regardless of what the Manager agent claims."

4. The "Zero-Knowledge" Sandbox

Because you are using OAuth 2.1 + DPoP, the Sandbox never actually "sees" your master keys.

The Sandbox generates a temporary Key Pair.

The Authorizer Agent takes the Sandbox's Public Key and wraps it into the Token.

The Token is only valid when signed by that Sandbox’s Private Key.

The Result: Even if an attacker "breaks out" of the sandbox, they have a token that is:

Scoped (Can only do one thing).

Short-lived (Expires in 5 minutes).

Bound (Useless without the VM's ephemeral private key).

The Strategy for your Business OS

You’ve already built the Orchestrator. Your next "2-day sprint" shouldn't be another integration; it should be this Authorizer Logic.

The Play:

Step A: Your Orchestrator creates the Intent node.

Step B: A "Reviewer" function (The Authorizer Agent) validates the Intent.

Step C: It triggers the brain start-sandbox command, passing in the scoped token.

Brutally Honest Take: This is the difference between a "Toy" and "Infrastructure." Paperclip is a toy because it trusts its agents. You are building infrastructure because you distrust them and verify their intent at the protocol level.

Would you like me to draft the "Judge's" System Prompt? It needs to be incredibly robust to avoid being "socially engineered" by the Worker agents. Conclude with a clear next step.

I just spent 2 days building an orchestrator for delegating tasks to local claude code using agents sdk. was this the wrong approach? should we just instead have made a generic provider interface that could hook up with third party tools
Geminis svar
You didn’t waste 2 days—you just finished the Logic Layer (the "What"). The "Generic Provider Interface" is the Plumbing Layer (the "Where").

In the context of a Business OS, building for Local Claude Code first is a classic "Local-First" development move. It's fast, cheap, and has zero latency. But if you stop there, you’ve built a powerful CLI tool, not an Autonomous Operating System.

Here is the brutal breakdown of why you are halfway to the right answer.

1. The "Orchestrator" vs. "Provider" Distinction

What you built: The logic of how to break a project into tasks, how to prompt Claude to execute them, and how to handle the "Agents SDK" callbacks.

What you're missing: An abstraction that says, "I don't care if this task runs on my MacBook, a Docker container, or an E2B sandbox."

Was it the wrong approach? No. You needed to prove the "Brain-to-Agent" handshake worked. Now that you have the orchestrator, you just need to wrap your execution call in an interface.

2. Why "Local-Only" is a Dead End for a Business OS

If your goal is a fully autonomous system (Sales, Support, Marketing), local execution fails for three reasons:

The "Always-On" Problem: A Business OS can't die when you close your laptop. It needs to live on a server (VPS/Cloud).

The Security Trap: If a "Marketing Agent" reads a malicious tweet or a "Support Agent" opens a poisoned PDF, and it’s running locally, that agent has access to your ~/.ssh keys and your browser cookies. This is why Paperclip is obsessed with #248 (Sandboxes).

Concurrency: A Business OS might need to run 10 agents simultaneously. Your local machine will melt.

3. The "2026 Strategy": The Hybrid Approach

You should pivot your orchestrator to use a Provider Pattern. Your 2 days of work becomes the "Local Provider" implementation.

The Architecture you should move toward:

TypeScript
interface ExecutionProvider {
  run(task: TaskNode, context: GraphContext): Promise<ObservationNode>;
}

// Your current work becomes this:
class LocalClaudeProvider implements ExecutionProvider { ... }

// Your future-proofing becomes these:
class E2BSandboxProvider implements ExecutionProvider { ... }
class DockerProvider implements ExecutionProvider { ... }
4. How this Leapfrogs Paperclip

Paperclip is building the Provider Interface first, but their Orchestrator is often just a "Loop."

Your Advantage: If your orchestrator is "Graph-Native" (reading/writing to SurrealDB), you are ahead.

The Play: Use your orchestrator to manage the state in the Brain, but let the Provider handle the "dirty work" of execution.

The Verdict: Keep the Logic, Abstract the Metal

Don't throw away your code. Refactor the part where you actually call claude-code into a separate class.

Why this is actually better: In 2026, Privacy is a product feature. For a Family Office, running a "Local Provider" on a dedicated Mac Mini in a closet is a huge selling point. For a Marketing agency, running "E2B Providers" in the cloud for scale is the play. By having an interface, you support both.

The "Brutally Honest" Reality Check: If you spent 2 days and the agent successfully finished a task and wrote the result back to your Graph, you’ve done more than 90% of the "AI wrapper" devs out there. You’ve built the connective tissue.

Open Source · Self-Hosted

The coordination layer
for AI agents

Your agents don't talk to each other. You're the one copy-pasting context between them. Brain is the self-correcting knowledge graph that replaces you as the integration layer — across engineering, sales, support, and operations.

Request Early Access See how it works
Architecture

MCP Protocol
The problem

You are the integration layer
between your AI agents

You already use AI agents. Your coding agent writes your code. Your chat assistant helps you think through architecture. Your editor agent autocompletes. But none of them share context.

  • → Copy error logs from CI into your coding agent
  • → Relay architecture decisions from a chat into your codebase
  • → Manually track what was decided, what's blocked, what changed
  • → Re-explain project state every time you start a new session
  • → Context-switch between tools, losing continuity at every step

Every agent you add makes it worse. Brain fixes this — not by replacing your agents, but by giving them shared memory.

The architecture

Why a graph, not a message bus

Most platforms try "agent swarms" — agents messaging agents. That creates a game of telephone where instructions get distorted. The graph is the single source of truth.

ApproachAgent swarms / message busesKnowledge graph (Brain)
LogicScripted workflows (if A, do B)State-based graph (emergent logic)
MemoryEphemeral, session-basedPersistent, pruned, versioned
CoordinationAgents message agents (telephone game)Agents read/write to shared truth
VerificationAssumes API calls workContinuous telemetry (reality grounding)
Autonomy"Let it rip" (high risk of loops)Authority scopes (risk-managed)
Over timePerformance degradesSystem gets smarter via learnings
Specialized agents

A coordinated workforce, not a chatbot

Each agent has a role, a domain, and authority scopes. They coordinate through the knowledge graph — not through you.

Architect

CTO / CPO

Technical decisions, system design, architecture constraints. Checks implementations against what was decided. Resolves conflicts between competing approaches.

Strategist

CEO / CSO

Market positioning, pricing, GTM, competitive response. Challenges product decisions against business viability. Catches positioning drift before it compounds.

Management

COO

Task tracking, priority management, execution velocity. Flags blocked work, stale decisions, and resource conflicts. Keeps the machine running.

Coding Agents

Via MCP · Cursor, Aider, Codex, and others

Your existing tools connected to the graph. Context injected on session start. Decisions, observations, and questions flow back automatically.

Design Partner

Early-stage co-designer

Brainstorms product ideas, asks probing questions, identifies gaps. Shapes vague ideas into structured projects, features, and decisions in the graph.

Observer

Background intelligence

Scans the graph for patterns nobody asked about. Stale decisions, cross-project conflicts, missing coverage, priority drift. Surfaces observations that compound into suggestions.

How it works

Agents coordinate through the graph

No agent messages another agent. They write structured signals to the knowledge graph. The graph makes it visible to the right agent at the right time.

1

Coding agent notices a contradiction

While implementing rate limiting, a coding agent detects that src/billing/api.ts uses REST — but the graph has a confirmed decision to standardize on tRPC. It logs an observation.

2

Architect agent sees it on next context load

The Architect checks the observation against constraints. Confirms the contradiction is real. Generates a suggestion: "Migrate billing API to tRPC, or revisit the standardization decision."

3

Suggestion surfaces in your feed

You see the suggestion with full provenance — the observation, the contradicted decision, the Architect's reasoning. You accept it. A migration task is created with one click.

4

Next coding session picks up the task

The migration task appears in the coding agent's context. It decomposes into subtasks, works through them, and status rolls up automatically. No human copied anything between tabs.

Connect in 60 seconds

Your coding agent, with context

One-time setup. Context injection is automatic on every session after that.

terminal
# One-time workspace setup
$ brain init
  Opens browser → authenticate → approve scopes
  ✓ Connected to workspace

# Start a task-scoped session
$ brain start task:implement-rate-limiting
  Context: 3 decisions, 2 constraints, 1 open question
  Task status: todo → in_progress

# Or just open your MCP-compatible coding agent
$ codex
  SessionStart → project context loaded
  4 decisions · 2 tasks · 1 recent observation
Key concepts

Built different

The primitives that make coordination possible.

⬡ Decisions

Every decision is tracked — who made it, why, and what alternatives were considered. Agents propose. Humans confirm. Nothing falls through the cracks.

◉ Observations

"I noticed X." Agents surface contradictions, gaps, patterns, and risks as they work. Observations accumulate and compound into actionable suggestions.

⊞ Projects, Features & Tasks

Work breaks down hierarchically. Agents decompose tasks at runtime. Status rolls up automatically. You see progress without tracking it.

✦ Suggestions

Agents tell you what you should be thinking about. Accept a suggestion and it becomes a task, decision, or feature — with full trace back to the evidence.

? Questions

When an agent doesn't know, it asks instead of guessing. You answer. The answer becomes a decision in the graph. No stalling, no silent assumptions.

💬 Conversations

Every chat produces structured knowledge. Conversations group by project automatically. Branch when topics diverge. Find anything by following the graph, not scrolling history.

⟠ Commits

Code is linked to the decisions and tasks it implements. Contradictions are caught before they land. Every change has a trail back to the intent behind it.

△ Authority Scopes

Control what each agent can do without asking. Start restrictive. Expand trust over time. You set the boundary — agents stay within it.

⬢ Intents

Every agent action starts as an intent — a structured request in the graph. Intents carry the full authorization context: what, why, and with what constraints. They're evaluated against authority scopes before execution.

🧠 Learnings

Agents that repeat mistakes are useless. Learnings are behavioral rules injected into agent prompts at runtime — created by humans directly or suggested by other agents. The system gets smarter as it works, not dumber.

◇ Living Descriptions

Descriptions update themselves. Ship a feature — the project summary reflects it. Confirm a decision — related entities incorporate it. No one writes status updates.

⟡ Cross-Project Intelligence

Conflicts between projects surface automatically. Duplicate work gets flagged. A decision in one project that breaks another is caught before anyone notices manually.

⊕ Agent Sessions

Every session is remembered. The next agent knows what the last one did — what was decided, what questions came up, what got built. Context carries forward.

⟐ Identity

One person across all tools. Your Slack, GitHub, and terminal sessions all resolve to the same identity. Agents act on your behalf with scoped permissions.

Reliability layer

Solving the three drifts

Autonomous systems don't fail from lack of intelligence. They fail from drift — slow divergence between what the system believes and what's actually true.

Context Drift

The graph becomes a junkyard of stale decisions

Decisions made in v1.0 become poison for v2.0. Brain uses temporal decay — nodes that aren't referenced lose weight over time. The Observer agent runs conflict resolution loops, flagging stale decisions that contradict recent commits. Context is filtered for relevance and recency, not dumped wholesale.

Authority Drift

Too autonomous = dangerous. Too locked down = a dashboard

Autonomy isn't binary. Brain uses tiered authority scopes — from zero-human atomic actions to multi-model consensus for high-stakes moves. Agents operate within risk budgets, not permission checkboxes. You manage thresholds, not individual approvals.

Reality Drift

The graph says 90% done, but the code is broken

If the Brain only reads its own graph, it's a delusion engine. Observer agents perform truth audits — checking claims against actual state via webhooks and integrations. When reality diverges from the graph, the system triggers a desync alert before any agent acts on stale data.

Beyond coding

From developer tool to company brain

The same graph that coordinates coding agents can coordinate entire departments. When Support logs an observation, Sales knows not to pitch an angry customer. No human relayed the message.

Engineering

Architecture, CI/CD, code quality

Coding agents, architecture decisions, commit-linked tasks. The foundation that's already working.

Growth

Marketing, content, SEO

Ad spend observations, conversion tracking, copy drafts. Features shipped by Engineering auto-surface for marketing.

Sales

Pipeline, outreach, contracts

CRM activity flows into the graph. Agents check customer sentiment before outreach. Deal claims verified against payment webhooks.

Support

Tickets, sentiment, escalation

Bug reports become observations. Repeated issues compound into feature suggestions. Support context flows to Engineering automatically.

Operations

Budget, vendors, compliance

Procurement tracked as intents with authority scopes. Budget decisions verified against Stripe. No shadow spending.

Strategy

Market intelligence, positioning

Competitive observations, pricing decisions, GTM plans. The Strategist challenges product decisions against business viability.

The competitive advantage isn't automating tasks — it's automating the context-sharing between human silos. When every department writes to the same graph, cross-departmental intelligence emerges for free.

Open source

Your coordination layer,
your code

The knowledge graph that coordinates your agents shouldn't be a black box you rent. It should be infrastructure you own, inspect, and extend.

◈
Full source accessGraph engine, MCP server, agent prompts, extraction pipeline. Every line.
◈
No vendor lock-inYour data lives in your SurrealDB instance. Export anytime. Migrate anytime.
◈
Extend everythingCustom agent types, observation categories, feed cards, MCP tools.
◈
Community-drivenAgent prompts, authority scope templates, and integrations contributed by users.
View on GitHub MIT License
stack
# The full stack
Graph        SurrealDB
Backend      Bun (Bun.serve) · TypeScript
Frontend     React · Tiptap · Reagraph
Auth         Better Auth · OAuth 2.1 · RAR · DPoP
LLM          Provider-agnostic (OpenRouter · Ollama · BYO keys)
Agents       MCP Server · Git Hooks
Protocol     MCP · OAuth 2.1 · RAR · DPoP · OIDC

# Lines of duct tape replaced
∞
Agentic authorization

Tokens that know
what they're for

Generic OAuth scopes like write:tasks aren't enough for autonomous agents. Brain's authorization server issues tokens that carry the full intent — what the agent wants to do, why, and with what constraints.

◈
Rich Authorization Requests (RAR)Tokens embed structured authorization_details from the knowledge graph. Not "scope: finance" — but "move $500 from Account A to Account B, authorized by Decision #47."
◈
DPoP Proof-of-PossessionEvery token is cryptographically bound to the agent's key. Stolen tokens are useless without the private key in the agent's isolated runtime.
◈
Intents as Authorization ContextAgent actions start as intents in the graph. The authorization server evaluates intents against authority scopes, then mints a token scoped to exactly that action.
◈
Better Auth as Identity ProviderHuman authentication (login, MFA, sessions) stays with Better Auth. The custom authorization server handles the agent side — token issuance, RAR scopes, DPoP binding.
authorization flow
# 1. Human authenticates via Better Auth
User  →  Better Auth (login, MFA)
        →  session cookie

# 2. Agent requests authorization with intent
Agent →  POST /authorize
        authorization_details: [{
          type: "intent",
          intent: "task:deploy-v2",
          actions: ["execute"],
          constraints: decision:d47
        }]

# 3. Token issued with DPoP binding
AS    →  Access Token (JWT)
        cnf: { jkt: "agent-key-thumbprint" }
        authorization_details: [...]

# 4. Agent proves possession on every request
Agent →  DPoP: signed-proof-jwt
        Authorization: DPoP access_token
Self-hosted

Your data never leaves your machine

Your decisions, architecture, strategy, and competitive intelligence stay on your infrastructure.

⬡

Docker Compose

SurrealDB + Bun + React. Running in 2 minutes on any machine.

docker compose up
◉

Single Binary

Embedded SurrealDB. No dependencies. Laptop, VPS, or Raspberry Pi.

brain serve
△

Cloud (Coming)

Managed hosting for teams. Same codebase. Your data, your region.

Waitlist open
Bring your own LLM keys Uses your provider keys (OpenRouter, Ollama, or direct). No data passes through Brain's servers.
Works offline Graph, feed, and local tools work without internet. LLM features can run offline with local models, or use networked providers when configured.
No telemetry No usage tracking. No analytics. No phone-home. Fully air-gappable.
Early access

Stop being the glue
between your agents

Brain is in early development. If you're running multiple AI agents across engineering, sales, or operations and drowning in context management, we want to talk.

Request Early Access
© 2026 Brain · Built by Marcus Schack
Architecture Article GitHub

content.text: Now add the "Auditable by design" section between Beyond Coding and Open Source:

input.old_string: The competitive advantage isn't automating tasks — it's automating the context-sharing between human silos. When every department writes to the same graph, cross-departmental intelligence emerges for free.
input.new_string:
The competitive advantage isn't automating tasks — it's automating the context-sharing between human silos. When every department writes to the same graph, cross-departmental intelligence emerges for free.

Verifiable autonomy

Auditable by design,
not by afterthought

Most autonomous platforms are black boxes. Brain is a signed logic trace. Every decision, every dollar, every line of code has a provenance chain back to the intent that authorized it.

Governance telemetry

Every decision is a node with a UUID, author, timestamp, and reasoning. Researchers and auditors can query the graph directly — no digging through chat logs or stdout dumps.

Signed intent chains

When an agent spends money or merges code, the graph records which intent authorized it, which authority scope permitted it, and which human (or consensus) approved it. The full chain is machine-readable.

Eval-ready metrics

Context retention, conflict resolution rate, authority distribution, decision velocity — all computable directly from the graph. No custom instrumentation required.

The "Judge" pattern

High-stakes actions go through an Authorizer Agent — a separate, privileged instance that validates intents against policy constraints before minting scoped tokens. The worker never sees master keys.

Protocol     MCP · OAuth 2.1 · RAR · DPoP · OIDC

# Lines of duct tape replaced
∞
Agentic authorization

Tokens that know
what they're for

Generic OAuth scopes like write:tasks aren't enough for autonomous agents. Brain's authorization server issues tokens that carry the full intent — what the agent wants to do, why, and with what constraints.

◈
Rich Authorization Requests (RAR)Tokens embed structured authorization_details from the knowledge graph. Not "scope: finance" — but "move $500 from Account A to Account B, authorized by Decision #47."
◈
DPoP Proof-of-PossessionEvery token is cryptographically bound to the agent's key. Stolen tokens are useless without the private key in the agent's isolated runtime.
◈
Intents as Authorization ContextAgent actions start as intents in the graph. The authorization server evaluates intents against authority scopes, then mints a token scoped to exactly that action.
◈
Better Auth as Identity ProviderHuman authentication (login, MFA, sessions) stays with Better Auth. The custom authorization server handles the agent side — token issuance, RAR scopes, DPoP binding.
authorization flow
# 1. Human authenticates via Better Auth
User  →  Better Auth (login, MFA)
        →  session cookie

# 2. Agent requests authorization with intent
Agent →  POST /authorize
        authorization_details: [{
          type: "intent",
          intent: "task:deploy-v2",
          actions: ["execute"],
          constraints: decision:d47
        }]

# 3. Token issued with DPoP binding
AS    →  Access Token (JWT)
        cnf: { jkt: "agent-key-thumbprint" }
        authorization_details: [...]

# 4. Agent proves possession on every request
Agent →  DPoP: signed-proof-jwt
        Authorization: DPoP access_token

Your data never leaves your machine

Your decisions, architecture, strategy, and competitive intelligence stay on your infrastructure.

⬡

Docker Compose

SurrealDB + Bun + React. Running in 2 minutes on any machine.

docker compose up
◉

Single Binary

Embedded SurrealDB. No dependencies. Laptop, VPS, or Raspberry Pi.

brain serve
△

Cloud (Coming)

Managed hosting for teams. Same codebase. Your data, your region.

Waitlist open
Bring your own LLM keys Uses your provider keys (OpenRouter, Ollama, or direct). No data passes through Brain's servers.
Works offline Graph, feed, and local tools work without internet. LLM features can run offline with local models, or use networked providers when configured.
No telemetry No usage tracking. No analytics. No phone-home. Fully air-gappable.

lines:
+

Most autonomous platforms are black boxes. Brain is a signed logic trace. Every decision, every dollar, every line of code has a provenance chain back to the intent that authorized it.

+

Every decision is a node with a UUID, author, timestamp, and reasoning. Researchers and auditors can query the graph directly — no digging through chat logs or stdout dumps.

+

When an agent spends money or merges code, the graph records which intent authorized it, which authority scope permitted it, and which human (or consensus) approved it. The full chain is machine-readable.

+

Context retention, conflict resolution rate, authority distribution, decision velocity — all computable directly from the graph. No custom instrumentation required.

+

High-stakes actions go through an Authorizer Agent — a separate, privileged instance that validates intents against policy constraints before minting scoped tokens. The worker never sees master keys.

Execution layer

Run anywhere. State lives in the graph.

The Brain is the director. Sandboxes are disposable. An agent can die every 5 seconds — the next one picks up exact context because state is external to the execution environment.

⊞

Local

Claude Code, Codex, Cursor — your existing tools on your machine, connected via MCP.

brain start task:xyz
◉

Sandboxed

E2B, Daytona, or Docker. The graph injects a scoped token — the sandbox never sees master keys.

Provider interface
△

Multi-model consensus

High-stakes moves verified by multiple models before execution. The Authorizer Agent mints tokens only when consensus clears.

Authority tier 3
Stateless sandboxes Observations and decisions write to SurrealDB in real-time. No VM persistence needed. Cheaper, more scalable, fully auditable.
Zero-knowledge execution Sandboxes generate ephemeral key pairs. Tokens are DPoP-bound to that key. Even a compromised sandbox can't escalate — the token is scoped, short-lived, and useless without the VM's private key.
Provider-agnostic The orchestrator manages state in the graph. The provider handles execution. Swap runtimes without changing logic. Local for privacy, cloud for scale.
Self-hosted

Brain
Problem Agents How it works Auth Open Source Article Request Access
Open Source · Self-Hosted

The coordination layer
for AI agents

Your agents don't talk to each other. You're the one copy-pasting context between them. Brain is the self-correcting knowledge graph that replaces you as the integration layer — across engineering, sales, support, and operations.

Request Early Access See how it works
Architecture
Human
Web Chat
Feed
Graph View

Observer
↕
